title: AP World History: Modern MCQ Practice Exam 11

questions.prompt: Originalized royal instruction, Goryeo Korea, c. 1250: “Let the district officials send grain records to the capital and let the monasteries pray for the protection of the realm. Scholars trained in the classics shall review petitions, while military commanders guard the northern roads.” What was the most likely purpose of the instruction?

choices.A: To present government as a system linking bureaucracy, Buddhist institutions, scholarship, and defense
choices.B: To abolish all written administration in Korea
choices.C: To place Korea under direct rule by Japanese shoguns
choices.D: To end the political role of scholars and officials

questions.correctAnswerText: To present government as a system linking bureaucracy, Buddhist institutions, scholarship, and defense
questions.explanation: The instruction connects officials, grain records, monasteries, scholars, petitions, commanders, and roads.

questions.prompt: Originalized royal instruction, Goryeo Korea, c. 1250: “Let the district officials send grain records to the capital and let the monasteries pray for the protection of the realm. Scholars trained in the classics shall review petitions, while military commanders guard the northern roads.” Which phrase best supports the claim that Buddhist institutions contributed to political legitimacy?

choices.A: “Military commanders”
choices.B: “Grain records”
choices.C: “Monasteries pray for the protection of the realm”
choices.D: “Scholars trained in the classics”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Monasteries pray for the protection of the realm”
questions.explanation: This phrase directly shows Buddhist institutions supporting the legitimacy and protection of the state.

questions.prompt: Originalized royal instruction, Goryeo Korea, c. 1250: “Let the district officials send grain records to the capital and let the monasteries pray for the protection of the realm. Scholars trained in the classics shall review petitions, while military commanders guard the northern roads.” Which broader context best explains the reference to grain records?

choices.A: East Asian states often used bureaucratic records to manage agrarian revenue and local administration.
choices.B: Korea had no agricultural taxation before 1450.
choices.C: Grain records were used only in Atlantic plantation colonies.
choices.D: Goryeo officials rejected written records as foreign technology.

questions.correctAnswerText: East Asian states often used bureaucratic records to manage agrarian revenue and local administration.
questions.explanation: Grain records sent by district officials show bureaucratic control over agrarian resources.

questions.prompt: Originalized royal instruction, Goryeo Korea, c. 1250: “Let the district officials send grain records to the capital and let the monasteries pray for the protection of the realm. Scholars trained in the classics shall review petitions, while military commanders guard the northern roads.” The passage most directly suggests which social arrangement?

choices.A: A state without religious, military, or scholarly specialists
choices.B: A merchant republic controlled only by overseas traders
choices.C: A hierarchy involving officials, monks, scholars, commanders, and rural producers
choices.D: A pastoral society without a capital city

questions.correctAnswerText: A hierarchy involving officials, monks, scholars, commanders, and rural producers
questions.explanation: The passage presents specialized roles within a layered political and social order.

questions.prompt: Secondary-source description of Māori communities in Aotearoa New Zealand before 1450: “Settlements included fortified pā on ridges, storage pits for kūmara, carved meeting spaces, and paths linking coastal fishing zones with inland gardens.” Which interpretation is best supported by the description?

choices.A: Māori communities adapted settlement, food storage, and defense to varied island environments.
choices.B: Māori societies were founded by European settlers before 1450.
choices.C: Māori communities avoided agriculture and fishing.
choices.D: Fortified pā were built only after industrial railways arrived.

questions.correctAnswerText: Māori communities adapted settlement, food storage, and defense to varied island environments.
questions.explanation: Fortified pā, storage pits, gardens, fishing zones, and paths show adaptation to local environments.

questions.prompt: Originalized Tamil merchant-guild inscription, Southeast Asian port, c. 1250: “The guild protects its warehouse, honors the temple with lamps, and pays the ruler’s harbor tax. Ships arrive with cloth, pepper, camphor, and bronze vessels, and disputes among merchants shall be settled by the elders.” What was the most likely purpose of the inscription?

choices.A: To announce that South Asian merchants avoided overseas ports
choices.B: To record the rights, religious patronage, taxes, and commercial order of a merchant community
choices.C: To abolish all temple donations by merchants
choices.D: To replace maritime commerce with pastoral nomadism

questions.correctAnswerText: To record the rights, religious patronage, taxes, and commercial order of a merchant community
questions.explanation: The inscription describes a guild warehouse, temple donations, harbor taxes, commodities, and dispute settlement.

questions.prompt: Originalized Tamil merchant-guild inscription, Southeast Asian port, c. 1250: “The guild protects its warehouse, honors the temple with lamps, and pays the ruler’s harbor tax. Ships arrive with cloth, pepper, camphor, and bronze vessels, and disputes among merchants shall be settled by the elders.” Which phrase best supports the claim that commerce and religious patronage were connected?

choices.A: “Honors the temple with lamps”
choices.B: “Ships arrive”
choices.C: “Harbor tax”
choices.D: “Settled by the elders”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Honors the temple with lamps”
questions.explanation: This phrase directly connects merchant activity to religious patronage.

questions.prompt: Originalized Tamil merchant-guild inscription, Southeast Asian port, c. 1250: “The guild protects its warehouse, honors the temple with lamps, and pays the ruler’s harbor tax. Ships arrive with cloth, pepper, camphor, and bronze vessels, and disputes among merchants shall be settled by the elders.” Which broader context best explains the ruler’s harbor tax?

choices.A: Southeast Asian rulers could profit from Indian Ocean trade by taxing ports and protecting merchant communities.
choices.B: Maritime taxes disappeared after 1200.
choices.C: South Asian merchants controlled Southeast Asian states directly.
choices.D: Harbor taxes were used only in Atlantic colonial ports.

questions.correctAnswerText: Southeast Asian rulers could profit from Indian Ocean trade by taxing ports and protecting merchant communities.
questions.explanation: The harbor tax reflects local rulers’ ability to collect revenue from overseas commerce.

questions.prompt: Originalized Tamil merchant-guild inscription, Southeast Asian port, c. 1250: “The guild protects its warehouse, honors the temple with lamps, and pays the ruler’s harbor tax. Ships arrive with cloth, pepper, camphor, and bronze vessels, and disputes among merchants shall be settled by the elders.” The inscription most directly illustrates which feature of Indian Ocean commerce?

choices.A: Merchant communities could maintain institutions abroad while operating within local political systems.
choices.B: Merchant guilds prevented cultural exchange across the Indian Ocean.
choices.C: Overseas merchants always ruled ports without local rulers.
choices.D: Indian Ocean trade ended the use of warehouses and contracts.

questions.correctAnswerText: Merchant communities could maintain institutions abroad while operating within local political systems.
questions.explanation: The guild protects its warehouse and settles disputes while paying the ruler’s harbor tax.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of the Arabian Sea c. 1300 shows seasonal arrows from Gujarat, Hormuz, Aden, and East African ports. Notes identify dhow routes, pearl banks, horse shipments, and monsoon wind timing.” Which conclusion is best supported by the map?

choices.A: Monsoon knowledge and sailing technology helped coordinate commerce across the Arabian Sea.
choices.B: Arabian Sea trade depended on steamships and telegraphs.
choices.C: Gujarat and Aden were isolated from maritime exchange.
choices.D: Horse shipments moved only by trans-Saharan caravan routes.

questions.correctAnswerText: Monsoon knowledge and sailing technology helped coordinate commerce across the Arabian Sea.
questions.explanation: The map emphasizes seasonal winds, dhow routes, ports, commodities, and timing.

questions.prompt: Originalized Qing frontier memorial, c. 1720: “Banner officers shall report on pasture disputes, and lamas at the frontier monastery shall receive gifts for prayers of loyalty. Mongol nobles who send tribute horses will keep their titles, but unauthorized tax demands on caravans are forbidden.” What was the most likely purpose of the memorial?

choices.A: To coordinate frontier administration through military officials, religious patronage, noble titles, and trade regulation
choices.B: To abolish all Mongol noble titles
choices.C: To end Qing involvement in Inner Asian frontier politics
choices.D: To replace caravan trade with Atlantic plantation labor

questions.correctAnswerText: To coordinate frontier administration through military officials, religious patronage, noble titles, and trade regulation
questions.explanation: The memorial links banner officers, lamas, Mongol nobles, tribute horses, and caravan taxation.

questions.prompt: Originalized Qing frontier memorial, c. 1720: “Banner officers shall report on pasture disputes, and lamas at the frontier monastery shall receive gifts for prayers of loyalty. Mongol nobles who send tribute horses will keep their titles, but unauthorized tax demands on caravans are forbidden.” Which phrase best supports the claim that religious patronage helped Qing frontier rule?

choices.A: “Pasture disputes”
choices.B: “Lamas at the frontier monastery shall receive gifts”
choices.C: “Tribute horses”
choices.D: “Unauthorized tax demands”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Lamas at the frontier monastery shall receive gifts”
questions.explanation: This phrase shows Qing patronage of Buddhist religious figures on the frontier.

questions.prompt: Originalized Qing frontier memorial, c. 1720: “Banner officers shall report on pasture disputes, and lamas at the frontier monastery shall receive gifts for prayers of loyalty. Mongol nobles who send tribute horses will keep their titles, but unauthorized tax demands on caravans are forbidden.” Which broader context best explains the reference to Mongol nobles keeping titles?

choices.A: Qing rulers often incorporated Inner Asian elites into imperial administration.
choices.B: Qing rulers eliminated all frontier elites after 1644.
choices.C: Mongol nobles were elected by European parliaments.
choices.D: Inner Asia had no political relationship with Qing rule.

questions.correctAnswerText: Qing rulers often incorporated Inner Asian elites into imperial administration.
questions.explanation: Allowing Mongol nobles to keep titles reflects incorporation of frontier elites.

questions.prompt: Originalized Qing frontier memorial, c. 1720: “Banner officers shall report on pasture disputes, and lamas at the frontier monastery shall receive gifts for prayers of loyalty. Mongol nobles who send tribute horses will keep their titles, but unauthorized tax demands on caravans are forbidden.” The memorial most directly suggests which relationship between state power and commerce?

choices.A: Imperial governments could regulate frontier trade while incorporating local elites.
choices.B: Caravans were irrelevant to Qing frontier policy.
choices.C: Frontier trade always operated without state oversight.
choices.D: Qing rulers rejected all tribute relationships.

questions.correctAnswerText: Imperial governments could regulate frontier trade while incorporating local elites.
questions.explanation: The memorial regulates unauthorized caravan taxes while recognizing Mongol nobles and tribute.

questions.prompt: Described Romanov service-nobility portrait, Russia, c. 1680: “A nobleman stands beside an estate register and a cavalry saber. Behind him, peasants plow fields near a wooden church, while a clerk records households for tax and military service.” Which interpretation is best supported by the portrait?

choices.A: Russian nobles gained status through service, landholding, and control over dependent peasants.
choices.B: Russian nobles rejected military service after 1600.
choices.C: Russian peasants controlled the tax registers.
choices.D: Romanov rule depended entirely on overseas colonies.

questions.correctAnswerText: Russian nobles gained status through service, landholding, and control over dependent peasants.
questions.explanation: The estate register, saber, clerk, peasants, and tax-service record all show service nobility.

questions.prompt: Described Romanov service-nobility portrait, Russia, c. 1680: “A nobleman stands beside an estate register and a cavalry saber. Behind him, peasants plow fields near a wooden church, while a clerk records households for tax and military service.” Which detail best supports the claim that peasants were tied to elite power?

choices.A: “A wooden church”
choices.B: “A cavalry saber”
choices.C: “Peasants plow fields”
choices.D: “A nobleman stands”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Peasants plow fields”
questions.explanation: Peasant agricultural labor in the nobleman’s estate setting supports the claim that elite power rested on dependent peasants.

questions.prompt: Described Romanov service-nobility portrait, Russia, c. 1680: “A nobleman stands beside an estate register and a cavalry saber. Behind him, peasants plow fields near a wooden church, while a clerk records households for tax and military service.” Which argument is best supported by the portrait?

choices.A: Romanov state-building connected elite service, agrarian control, taxation, and military obligation.
choices.B: Russian state power declined because nobles refused service.
choices.C: Estate records show that Russia lacked bureaucracy.
choices.D: Peasant labor was unrelated to noble status.

questions.correctAnswerText: Romanov state-building connected elite service, agrarian control, taxation, and military obligation.
questions.explanation: The portrait combines estate records, cavalry service, peasant labor, household records, and taxation.

questions.prompt: Originalized French colonial ordinance, Caribbean, c. 1685: “The enslaved shall be baptized and instructed, but those who run away shall be punished. Masters must provide food and clothing, and colonial judges shall record penalties so that plantation order is preserved.” What was the most likely purpose of the ordinance?

choices.A: To regulate enslaved labor and social control in a plantation colony
choices.B: To abolish slavery throughout the French empire
choices.C: To give enslaved people equal citizenship rights
choices.D: To end plantation production in the Caribbean

questions.correctAnswerText: To regulate enslaved labor and social control in a plantation colony
questions.explanation: The ordinance concerns enslaved people, punishment, masters, food, clothing, judges, and plantation order.

questions.prompt: Originalized French colonial ordinance, Caribbean, c. 1685: “The enslaved shall be baptized and instructed, but those who run away shall be punished. Masters must provide food and clothing, and colonial judges shall record penalties so that plantation order is preserved.” Which phrase best supports the claim that coercion was built into the legal system?

choices.A: “Baptized and instructed”
choices.B: “Colonial judges shall record penalties”
choices.C: “Provide food and clothing”
choices.D: “Plantation order”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Colonial judges shall record penalties”
questions.explanation: This phrase shows coercion being formalized through colonial legal procedures.

questions.prompt: Originalized French colonial ordinance, Caribbean, c. 1685: “The enslaved shall be baptized and instructed, but those who run away shall be punished. Masters must provide food and clothing, and colonial judges shall record penalties so that plantation order is preserved.” Which broader context best explains the reference to baptism and instruction?

choices.A: European colonial powers often used Christianity to justify and regulate social order in slave societies.
choices.B: Plantation colonies rejected all missionary activity.
choices.C: Caribbean colonies were governed by Buddhist monasteries.
choices.D: Baptism ended enslaved status automatically throughout the Atlantic world.

questions.correctAnswerText: European colonial powers often used Christianity to justify and regulate social order in slave societies.
questions.explanation: Baptism and instruction are presented alongside discipline and plantation control.

questions.prompt: Originalized French colonial ordinance, Caribbean, c. 1685: “The enslaved shall be baptized and instructed, but those who run away shall be punished. Masters must provide food and clothing, and colonial judges shall record penalties so that plantation order is preserved.” Which statement best evaluates the ordinance?

choices.A: It shows that plantation economies relied on legal systems that combined labor discipline, religious instruction, and master authority.
choices.B: It proves that slavery was based entirely on voluntary labor contracts.
choices.C: It argues that colonial judges had no role in plantation society.
choices.D: It shows that religion made plantation discipline unnecessary.

questions.correctAnswerText: It shows that plantation economies relied on legal systems that combined labor discipline, religious instruction, and master authority.
questions.explanation: The ordinance combines Christian instruction, punishment, master obligations, judges, and plantation order.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of the North Atlantic c. 1500–1700 shows Basque, Portuguese, English, and French ships sailing to Grand Banks fishing grounds. Shore stations dry cod, and arrows mark salted fish moving to Iberian, Caribbean, and Mediterranean markets.” Which conclusion is best supported by the map?

choices.A: North Atlantic fisheries connected maritime labor, food preservation, and transoceanic markets.
choices.B: Cod fishing had no relationship to Atlantic commerce.
choices.C: The Grand Banks were used only by inland caravan merchants.
choices.D: Salted fish moved only within local villages.

questions.correctAnswerText: North Atlantic fisheries connected maritime labor, food preservation, and transoceanic markets.
questions.explanation: Ships, fishing grounds, shore drying stations, salted cod, and distant markets all appear on the map.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of the North Atlantic c. 1500–1700 shows Basque, Portuguese, English, and French ships sailing to Grand Banks fishing grounds. Shore stations dry cod, and arrows mark salted fish moving to Iberian, Caribbean, and Mediterranean markets.” Which feature best supports the claim that preservation technology shaped trade?

choices.A: “Basque, Portuguese, English, and French ships”
choices.B: “Shore stations dry cod”
choices.C: “Grand Banks fishing grounds”
choices.D: “Caribbean and Mediterranean markets”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Shore stations dry cod”
questions.explanation: Drying cod was a preservation technique that made long-distance fish trade possible.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of the North Atlantic c. 1500–1700 shows Basque, Portuguese, English, and French ships sailing to Grand Banks fishing grounds. Shore stations dry cod, and arrows mark salted fish moving to Iberian, Caribbean, and Mediterranean markets.” The trade shown most directly illustrates which broader process?

choices.A: Oceanic expansion created commodity networks involving extraction, preservation, and distant consumption.
choices.B: Atlantic commerce was limited to silver and enslaved labor.
choices.C: European fishing communities avoided overseas markets.
choices.D: Maritime food production ended after 1500.

questions.correctAnswerText: Oceanic expansion created commodity networks involving extraction, preservation, and distant consumption.
questions.explanation: The map shows fishing, drying, salting, shipping, and consumption across multiple Atlantic and Mediterranean markets.

questions.prompt: Originalized Indian National Congress petition, c. 1885: “We ask not disorder but representation. If the empire claims justice, Indian voices must enter councils that decide taxation, examinations, and public works. Education has prepared loyal subjects to speak as citizens.” Which audience was the petition most likely intended to influence?

choices.A: British officials and educated Indian political reformers
choices.B: Mongol cavalry commanders
choices.C: Spanish planters in the Caribbean
choices.D: Cold War oil ministers

questions.correctAnswerText: British officials and educated Indian political reformers
questions.explanation: The petition addresses empire, councils, taxation, examinations, public works, education, and representation.

questions.prompt: Originalized Indian National Congress petition, c. 1885: “We ask not disorder but representation. If the empire claims justice, Indian voices must enter councils that decide taxation, examinations, and public works. Education has prepared loyal subjects to speak as citizens.” Which phrase best supports the claim that the petition linked education to political participation?

choices.A: “We ask not disorder”
choices.B: “Education has prepared loyal subjects to speak as citizens”
choices.C: “Public works”
choices.D: “The empire claims justice”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Education has prepared loyal subjects to speak as citizens”
questions.explanation: This phrase directly links education with political participation.

questions.prompt: Originalized Indian National Congress petition, c. 1885: “We ask not disorder but representation. If the empire claims justice, Indian voices must enter councils that decide taxation, examinations, and public works. Education has prepared loyal subjects to speak as citizens.” Which broader context best explains the petition?

choices.A: Educated colonial elites used liberal political language to demand greater participation in imperial governance.
choices.B: Indian political organizations rejected all forms of representation.
choices.C: British India had no educated urban elites after 1850.
choices.D: The petition reflected decolonization after World War II.

questions.correctAnswerText: Educated colonial elites used liberal political language to demand greater participation in imperial governance.
questions.explanation: The petition uses representation, justice, citizenship, and councils to ask for inclusion.

questions.prompt: Originalized Indian National Congress petition, c. 1885: “We ask not disorder but representation. If the empire claims justice, Indian voices must enter councils that decide taxation, examinations, and public works. Education has prepared loyal subjects to speak as citizens.” Which statement best evaluates the petition’s argument?

choices.A: It rejects political participation as dangerous to educated Indians.
choices.B: It argues that imperial claims of justice require representative inclusion of Indian voices.
choices.C: It claims taxation should be decided only by military commanders.
choices.D: It calls for immediate abolition of all councils.

questions.correctAnswerText: It argues that imperial claims of justice require representative inclusion of Indian voices.
questions.explanation: The petition challenges the empire to match its claims of justice with Indian participation in councils.

questions.prompt: Originalized abolitionist pamphlet, Britain, c. 1790: “The sugar in the teacup is sweetened by chains. Let households refuse slave-grown goods and let Parliament hear that Christian conscience and natural rights condemn the trade in human beings.” What was the most likely purpose of the pamphlet?

choices.A: To defend the Atlantic slave trade as morally necessary
choices.B: To encourage consumers and lawmakers to oppose slavery and the slave trade
choices.C: To argue that sugar had no connection to coerced labor
choices.D: To promote plantation expansion in the Caribbean

questions.correctAnswerText: To encourage consumers and lawmakers to oppose slavery and the slave trade
questions.explanation: The pamphlet urges households to refuse slave-grown goods and Parliament to act.

questions.prompt: Originalized abolitionist pamphlet, Britain, c. 1790: “The sugar in the teacup is sweetened by chains. Let households refuse slave-grown goods and let Parliament hear that Christian conscience and natural rights condemn the trade in human beings.” Which phrase best supports the claim that consumer behavior was used as a political tool?

choices.A: “Christian conscience”
choices.B: “Let households refuse slave-grown goods”
choices.C: “Natural rights”
choices.D: “The trade in human beings”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Let households refuse slave-grown goods”
questions.explanation: This phrase directly shows consumer boycott as a political tool.

questions.prompt: Originalized abolitionist pamphlet, Britain, c. 1790: “The sugar in the teacup is sweetened by chains. Let households refuse slave-grown goods and let Parliament hear that Christian conscience and natural rights condemn the trade in human beings.” Which broader ideological context best explains the pamphlet’s language?

choices.A: Abolitionism drew on religious morality and Enlightenment ideas about natural rights.
choices.B: Abolitionists rejected all moral arguments.
choices.C: Enlightenment thinkers universally defended plantation slavery.
choices.D: Consumer boycotts appeared only after the Cold War.

questions.correctAnswerText: Abolitionism drew on religious morality and Enlightenment ideas about natural rights.
questions.explanation: The pamphlet invokes Christian conscience and natural rights against slavery.

questions.prompt: Originalized abolitionist pamphlet, Britain, c. 1790: “The sugar in the teacup is sweetened by chains. Let households refuse slave-grown goods and let Parliament hear that Christian conscience and natural rights condemn the trade in human beings.” The pamphlet most directly connects social reform to which political institution?

choices.A: Parliament as a target for public pressure and legal change
choices.B: A nomadic council regulating caravan tolls
choices.C: A colonial mission town in Paraguay
choices.D: A Cold War international space agency

questions.correctAnswerText: Parliament as a target for public pressure and legal change
questions.explanation: The pamphlet urges Parliament to hear anti-slavery arguments, linking social activism to lawmaking.

questions.prompt:
Table: Teak extraction in British Burma, simplified pattern
Period | Reported development
1850s | Forest leases expand along river valleys
1880s | Steam sawmills and river transport increase timber exports
1900s | Officials create reserved forests and restrict village use
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Colonial rule linked resource extraction, industrial technology, and environmental regulation.
choices.B: Teak exports declined because steam technology disappeared.
choices.C: British officials avoided forest regulation in Burma.
choices.D: Village use of forests expanded without restriction after 1900.

questions.correctAnswerText: Colonial rule linked resource extraction, industrial technology, and environmental regulation.
questions.explanation: The table connects forest leases, steam sawmills, timber exports, reserved forests, and restrictions on village use.

questions.prompt:
Table: Teak extraction in British Burma, simplified pattern
Period | Reported development
1850s | Forest leases expand along river valleys
1880s | Steam sawmills and river transport increase timber exports
1900s | Officials create reserved forests and restrict village use

choices.A: “Forest leases”
choices.B: “Steam sawmills and river transport increase timber exports”
choices.C: “Reserved forests”
choices.D: “Restrict village use”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Steam sawmills and river transport increase timber exports”

questions.prompt:
Table: Teak extraction in British Burma, simplified pattern
Period | Reported development
1850s | Forest leases expand along river valleys

choices.A: Colonial states often claimed environmental management authority to control resources and local communities.
choices.B: Colonial governments abandoned all control over natural resources.
choices.C: Reserved forests were created by medieval merchant guilds.
choices.D: Village communities gained unrestricted legal control over timber exports.

questions.correctAnswerText: Colonial states often claimed environmental management authority to control resources and local communities.

questions.prompt:
Table: Teak extraction in British Burma, simplified pattern
Period | Reported development
1850s | Forest leases expand along river valleys
1880s | Steam sawmills and river transport increase timber exports
1900s | Officials create reserved forests and restrict village use
Which argument is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Imperial resource extraction could produce both export growth and new restrictions on local livelihoods.
choices.B: Steam technology reduced colonial interest in tropical timber.
choices.C: Forest regulation always increased village autonomy.
choices.D: Colonial extraction was unrelated to environmental change.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of Peru’s coast c. 1840–1880 marks guano islands, export ports, shipping routes to Europe and North America, and railroad lines financed by guano revenue but concentrated near mining and port zones.” Which conclusion is best supported by the map?

choices.A: Guano exports connected coastal environments, foreign demand, state revenue, and infrastructure projects.
choices.B: Guano trade remained entirely local within Peru.
choices.C: Railroads in Peru were unrelated to export revenue.
choices.D: Foreign demand for fertilizer disappeared before 1840.

questions.correctAnswerText: Guano exports connected coastal environments, foreign demand, state revenue, and infrastructure projects.
questions.explanation: The map links guano islands, ports, shipping routes, foreign markets, revenue, and railroads.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of Peru’s coast c. 1840–1880 marks guano islands, export ports, shipping routes to Europe and North America, and railroad lines financed by guano revenue but concentrated near mining and port zones.” Which feature best supports the claim that a coastal environmental resource became globally valuable?

choices.A: “Railroad lines”
choices.B: “Guano islands”
choices.C: “Mining and port zones”
choices.D: “Peru’s coast”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Guano islands”
questions.explanation: Guano islands identify the coastal environmental resource that became valuable in global markets.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of Peru’s coast c. 1840–1880 marks guano islands, export ports, shipping routes to Europe and North America, and railroad lines financed by guano revenue but concentrated near mining and port zones.” The pattern shown most directly connects guano exports to which technological or economic development?

choices.A: Demand for fertilizer in expanding commercial agriculture
choices.B: The spread of digital communication networks
choices.C: The decline of all oceanic shipping
choices.D: The use of gunpowder in land-based empires

questions.correctAnswerText: Demand for fertilizer in expanding commercial agriculture
questions.explanation: Guano became valuable because commercial agriculture required fertilizer.

questions.prompt: Described map: “A map of Peru’s coast c. 1840–1880 marks guano islands, export ports, shipping routes to Europe and North America, and railroad lines financed by guano revenue but concentrated near mining and port zones.” Which argument is best supported by the map?

choices.A: Export booms could strengthen state revenue while producing uneven regional infrastructure.
choices.B: Guano wealth eliminated Peru’s dependence on global markets.
choices.C: Railroad construction was evenly distributed across all rural communities.
choices.D: Peru’s government avoided using commodity revenue for infrastructure.

questions.correctAnswerText: Export booms could strengthen state revenue while producing uneven regional infrastructure.
questions.explanation: Guano revenue financed railroads concentrated around mining and port zones.

questions.prompt: Described fascist youth poster, Italy, 1930s: “Uniformed boys march beneath Roman eagles while a leader’s portrait watches from above. The caption reads, ‘Discipline, Nation, Empire.’ Girls in matching uniforms hold flags near a classroom map of the Mediterranean.” Which interpretation is best supported by the poster?

choices.A: Fascist regimes used youth organizations, classical symbolism, and education to promote militarized nationalism.
choices.B: Fascist movements rejected nationalism and empire.
choices.C: The poster criticizes dictatorship from a democratic perspective.
choices.D: Italian youth organizations avoided political symbolism.

questions.correctAnswerText: Fascist regimes used youth organizations, classical symbolism, and education to promote militarized nationalism.
questions.explanation: Uniformed youth, Roman eagles, the leader’s portrait, flags, classroom map, and empire slogan support the interpretation.

questions.prompt: Described fascist youth poster, Italy, 1930s: “Uniformed boys march beneath Roman eagles while a leader’s portrait watches from above. The caption reads, ‘Discipline, Nation, Empire.’ Girls in matching uniforms hold flags near a classroom map of the Mediterranean.” Which detail best supports the claim that the poster promoted authoritarian political loyalty?

choices.A: “A classroom map”
choices.B: “Roman eagles”
choices.C: “A leader’s portrait watches from above”
choices.D: “Girls in matching uniforms”

questions.correctAnswerText: “A leader’s portrait watches from above”
questions.explanation: The leader’s elevated image promotes authoritarian loyalty and surveillance.

questions.prompt: Described fascist youth poster, Italy, 1930s: “Uniformed boys march beneath Roman eagles while a leader’s portrait watches from above. The caption reads, ‘Discipline, Nation, Empire.’ Girls in matching uniforms hold flags near a classroom map of the Mediterranean.” Which broader context best explains the poster?

choices.A: Interwar authoritarian movements used mass culture and youth mobilization to build loyalty to the state.
choices.B: Fascist regimes avoided schools and propaganda.
choices.C: European states abandoned empire after World War I.
choices.D: Youth organizations disappeared from politics after 1900.

questions.correctAnswerText: Interwar authoritarian movements used mass culture and youth mobilization to build loyalty to the state.
questions.explanation: Fascist youth imagery, education, discipline, empire, and leader symbolism fit interwar authoritarian propaganda.

questions.prompt:
Table: International responses to Italy’s invasion of Ethiopia, simplified pattern
Action | Result
League condemns invasion | Moral criticism but limited enforcement
Sanctions exclude key materials such as oil | Limited pressure on Italy
Major powers avoid full military confrontation | Italy continues conquest
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: International institutions struggled to stop aggression when major powers avoided strong enforcement.
choices.B: The League of Nations quickly defeated Italy militarily.
choices.C: Sanctions completely ended Italy’s invasion.
choices.D: Major powers unanimously supported Ethiopian independence with military force.

questions.correctAnswerText: International institutions struggled to stop aggression when major powers avoided strong enforcement.
questions.explanation: The table shows condemnation, weak sanctions, avoidance of military confrontation, and continued conquest.

questions.prompt:
Table: International responses to Italy’s invasion of Ethiopia, simplified pattern
Action | Result
League condemns invasion | Moral criticism but limited enforcement
Sanctions exclude key materials such as oil | Limited pressure on Italy
Major powers avoid full military confrontation | Italy continues conquest
Which statement best evaluates the pattern shown?

choices.A: Collective security failed when condemnation was not matched by decisive enforcement.
choices.B: International law had no relationship to interwar diplomacy.
choices.C: Sanctions were unnecessary because Italy withdrew immediately.
choices.D: The League’s response ended all imperial expansion in Africa.

questions.correctAnswerText: Collective security failed when condemnation was not matched by decisive enforcement.
questions.explanation: The League condemned aggression but lacked effective sanctions or military backing.

questions.prompt: Originalized Yugoslav nonalignment speech, c. 1961: “We will not exchange one master for another by joining a military bloc. Small and newly independent states must trade, build, and speak with both sides while keeping the right to choose their own road.” What was the most likely purpose of the speech?

choices.A: To reject military blocs and defend independent foreign policy for smaller states
choices.B: To call for direct colonial rule to return
choices.C: To demand that all states join one superpower alliance
choices.D: To deny that newly independent states should trade internationally

questions.correctAnswerText: To reject military blocs and defend independent foreign policy for smaller states
questions.explanation: The speech opposes military blocs and defends the right of smaller and newly independent states to choose their own path.

questions.prompt: Originalized Yugoslav nonalignment speech, c. 1961: “We will not exchange one master for another by joining a military bloc. Small and newly independent states must trade, build, and speak with both sides while keeping the right to choose their own road.” Which phrase best supports the claim that the speaker valued autonomy from superpower control?

choices.A: “Trade, build, and speak”
choices.B: “Joining a military bloc”
choices.C: “The right to choose their own road”
choices.D: “Newly independent states”

questions.correctAnswerText: “The right to choose their own road”
questions.explanation: This phrase directly expresses political autonomy from superpower control.

questions.prompt: Originalized Yugoslav nonalignment speech, c. 1961: “We will not exchange one master for another by joining a military bloc. Small and newly independent states must trade, build, and speak with both sides while keeping the right to choose their own road.” Which broader context best explains the speech?

choices.A: Cold War rivalry created pressure on states to align with superpower blocs, while some leaders promoted nonalignment.
choices.B: The Cold War ended before 1950.
choices.C: Newly independent states had no role in international diplomacy.
choices.D: Military blocs were created only in the nineteenth century.

questions.correctAnswerText: Cold War rivalry created pressure on states to align with superpower blocs, while some leaders promoted nonalignment.
questions.explanation: The speech responds to military blocs and argues for independent diplomacy.

questions.prompt: Described Prague Spring poster, Czechoslovakia, 1968: “A printing press releases papers reading ‘socialism with a human face.’ A student holds a radio, workers gather in a square, and a tank silhouette appears at the edge of the image.” Which interpretation is best supported by the poster?

choices.A: Reform movements within communist states could use media, students, and workers to demand political liberalization.
choices.B: Communist governments in Eastern Europe had no internal reform movements.
choices.C: The poster supports censorship and military occupation.
choices.D: Students and workers avoided politics during the Cold War.

questions.correctAnswerText: Reform movements within communist states could use media, students, and workers to demand political liberalization.
questions.explanation: The printing press, student, radio, workers, and reform slogan all support this interpretation.

questions.prompt: Described Prague Spring poster, Czechoslovakia, 1968: “A printing press releases papers reading ‘socialism with a human face.’ A student holds a radio, workers gather in a square, and a tank silhouette appears at the edge of the image.” The poster most directly reflects which broader Cold War pattern?

choices.A: The disappearance of Soviet influence in Eastern Europe before 1968
choices.B: The use of military force and censorship to prevent all political change globally
choices.C: The tension between reformist socialism and Soviet-bloc control
choices.D: The end of student activism after World War II

questions.correctAnswerText: The tension between reformist socialism and Soviet-bloc control
questions.explanation: The reform slogan and tank silhouette show political liberalization confronting Soviet-bloc military pressure.

questions.prompt:
Table: Global tourism and heritage sites, simplified pattern
Indicator | c. 1980 | c. 2020
International tourist arrivals | Hundreds of millions | More than one billion
Heritage-site management | Mostly national concern | Increasingly tied to global tourism agencies and local communities
Common concern | Site preservation | Preservation, local livelihoods, crowding, and cultural ownership
Which conclusion is best supported by the table?

choices.A: Tourism globalization expanded economic opportunities while creating preservation and local-control debates.
choices.B: International tourism disappeared after 1980.
choices.C: Heritage sites became unrelated to local communities.
choices.D: Global tourism ended concerns about cultural ownership.

questions.correctAnswerText: Tourism globalization expanded economic opportunities while creating preservation and local-control debates.
questions.explanation: The table shows rising arrivals, global tourism agencies, local communities, livelihoods, crowding, and cultural ownership concerns.

questions.prompt:
Table: Global tourism and heritage sites, simplified pattern
Indicator | c. 1980 | c. 2020
International tourist arrivals | Hundreds of millions | More than one billion
Heritage-site management | Mostly national concern | Increasingly tied to global tourism agencies and local communities
Common concern | Site preservation | Preservation, local livelihoods, crowding, and cultural ownership
Which evidence best supports the claim that heritage became a global cultural issue?

choices.A: “Mostly national concern”
choices.B: “Increasingly tied to global tourism agencies and local communities”
choices.C: “Hundreds of millions”
choices.D: “Site preservation”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Increasingly tied to global tourism agencies and local communities”
questions.explanation: This phrase shows heritage becoming part of global cultural and institutional networks.

questions.prompt: Originalized statement by a heritage-site resident, c. 2020: “Visitors photograph our rituals, but rent rises faster than wages and shops sell souvenirs where neighbors once bought bread. We welcome guests, yet heritage should not turn a living town into a stage.” What was the most likely purpose of the statement?

choices.A: To argue that tourism should ignore local residents
choices.B: To criticize the social effects of mass tourism while accepting cultural exchange
choices.C: To demand that all heritage sites close permanently
choices.D: To deny that tourism affects urban economies

questions.correctAnswerText: To criticize the social effects of mass tourism while accepting cultural exchange
questions.explanation: The resident welcomes guests but criticizes rising rents, souvenir shops, and turning a town into a stage.

questions.prompt: Originalized statement by a heritage-site resident, c. 2020: “Visitors photograph our rituals, but rent rises faster than wages and shops sell souvenirs where neighbors once bought bread. We welcome guests, yet heritage should not turn a living town into a stage.” Which phrase best supports the claim that tourism changed local economic life?

choices.A: “Visitors photograph”
choices.B: “Rent rises faster than wages”
choices.C: “We welcome guests”
choices.D: “Our rituals”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Rent rises faster than wages”
questions.explanation: This phrase directly shows local economic pressure caused by tourism.

questions.prompt: Originalized statement by a heritage-site resident, c. 2020: “Visitors photograph our rituals, but rent rises faster than wages and shops sell souvenirs where neighbors once bought bread. We welcome guests, yet heritage should not turn a living town into a stage.” Which statement best evaluates the resident’s argument?

choices.A: The resident frames global tourism as culturally valuable but socially disruptive when local control is weakened.
choices.B: The resident argues that heritage has no economic value.
choices.C: The resident claims that cultural rituals should be controlled only by foreign tourists.
choices.D: The resident rejects all forms of cultural exchange.

questions.correctAnswerText: The resident frames global tourism as culturally valuable but socially disruptive when local control is weakened.
questions.explanation: The statement welcomes visitors while criticizing displacement, commercialization, and loss of lived community control.
